coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermally synthesized fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for catalytic efficiency and anti-counterfeiting application
coredata.dc:creator: Alam, Mir Waqas
coredata.dc:description:
Here, Tylophora indica plant extract was utilized to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal method. The synthesized NDCDs was employed to anti-counterfeiting and enhancing the catalytic activity of Malachite Green dye. The ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy and FT-IR measurements was used to analyze the optical properties and the development of NDCDs as well as the source of Tylophora indica. Using HR-TEM, XRD, and XPS, the structure and elemental contents of the produced NDCDs was investigated. The HRTEM images showed that the NDCDs had a d-spacing of 0.37 nm and an average particle size of 4.3 ± 1 nm. After being subjected to UV light at 365 nm, the produced NDCDs began to glow blue, with a quantum yield (QY) of 16.1 %. The I
D and I
G ratio values for NDCDs were found to be 0.86. With rate constant values calculated at 0.1830 min−1, respectively, the synthesized NDCDs were found to be a viable catalyst for the Malachite Green (MG) dye reduction process utilizing freshly prepared NaBH4. In addition, the NDCDs solution is used as fluorescent ink. The printed patterns retain their anti-counterfeiting properties even after being heated and stored in an environment for an extended period, indicating the NDCDs stability for prospective anti-counterfeiting applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal method
Quantum yield
Malachite green
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Evaluation of the catalytic activity of synthesized NDCDs
Morphology and surface state of NDCDs
Possible reduction mechanism of malachite green
Photostability of the NDCDs fluorescent ink

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Deanship of Scientific Research, King Saud University

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Vice Presidency for Graduate Studies and Scientific Research

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Deanship of Scientific Research, Vice Presidency for Graduate Studies and Scientific Research, King Faisal University, Saudi Arabia [Project No: GRANT5427].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) HRTEM image at 100 nm, (b) Particle size histogram of NDCDs, (c) Lattice fringes at 1 nm scale bar, (d) SAED pattern of NDCDs.
(a) UV–visible absorbance spectra of NDCDs, (b) FL excitation spectrum of NDCDs, (c) different excitation wavelengths.
The XRD patterns of the NDCDs.
(a) XPS full survey spectrum of NDCDs, (b) C1s; (c) N1s, (d) O1s.

UV spectra of Malachite Green with NaBH4, (b) UV spectra of Malachite Green by NaBH4 in the presence of the NDCDs catalyst, (c) Kinetics plot for the reduction of Malachite Green, (d) Photographs of before and after malachite green.
Graphical representation of Malachite green dye reduction mechanism.
Schematic representation of before and after malachite green.

(a) A “flower” design printed on a piece of non-fluorescent paper is encrypted and decrypted to provide anti-counterfeiting information. (b) A fluorescent “flower” design kept at room temperature for 30 days was subjected to an environmental stability test. (c) A fluorescent “flower” pattern's heat resistance test was conducted both before and after thermal treatment at 60 °C. All fluorescent designs are displayed under a UV lamp (365 nm) after being printed using the NDCD solutions as inks.
Photostability of NDCDs fluorescent ink; Inset figure (a) without UV light, (b) with UV light.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermally synthesized fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for catalytic efficiency and anti-counterfeiting application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, College of Science, King Faisal University, Al-Ahsa, 31982, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, College of Science, King Faisal University, Al-Ahsa, 31982, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Here, Tylophora indica plant extract was utilized to create nitrogen-doped carbon dots by hydrothermal method. The synthesized NDCDs was employed to anti-counterfeiting and enhancing the catalytic activity of Malachite Green dye. The ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy and FT-IR measurements was used to analyze the optical properties and the development of NDCDs as well as the source of Tylophora indica. Using HR-TEM, XRD, and XPS, the structure and elemental contents of the produced NDCDs was investigated. The HRTEM images showed that the NDCDs had a d-spacing of 0.37 nm and an average particle size of 4.3 ± 1 nm. After being subjected to UV light at 365 nm, the produced NDCDs began to glow blue, with a quantum yield (QY) of 16.1 %. The I
D and I
G ratio values for NDCDs were found to be 0.86. With rate constant values calculated at 0.1830 min−1, respectively, the synthesized NDCDs were found to be a viable catalyst for the Malachite Green (MG) dye reduction process utilizing freshly prepared NaBH4. In addition, the NDCDs solution is used as fluorescent ink. The printed patterns retain their anti-counterfeiting properties even after being heated and stored in an environment for an extended period, indicating the NDCDs stability for prospective anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination is a worldwide problem that has drawn much attention, especially with dyes [1]. The food, paper, rubber, textile, leather, cosmetics, and plastics industries all utilize a lot of organic dyes. Water treatment systems take into account a wide range of contaminants found in natural water sources while using organic dyes. The bulk of organic pigments are soluble in synthetic solvents, which are equally toxic to living things. Dyes are used extensively in the textile industry, especially malachite green (MG) dye, which has serious negative effects on the environment and human health. Wastewater treatment has therefore, become more and more important. Wastewater is treated using a variety of methods, such as photocatalysis, sedimentation, electrochemical porosis, reverse osmosis, and filtering. This technique, photocatalytic degradation, has attracted much interest because of its quick degradation, low toxicity, and affordability [2–5].

Since counterfeiting is a global issue that seriously jeopardizes people's lives, social order, and even national security, anti-counterfeiting materials and related encryption/decryption technologies have been developed in recent decades [6]. Luminous patterns, laser holograms, watermarks, and other anti-counterfeiting methods and materials have all been studied and applied to address the problems caused by counterfeit goods or incorrect information. Generally, the encryption and decryption procedures are absent from stored data. Furthermore, because they are so well-known, these conventional anti-counterfeiting tactics are simple to copy and helpless against the increasing array of fake approaches. Thus, in order to provide stored information with increased security, innovative anti-counterfeiting materials and creative encryption/decryption techniques must be developed immediately [7].

Carbon-based materials have drawn global attention recently and have supplanted a number of inorganic materials in dye and removal of heavy metals processes [8]. In the past few years, a wide range of carbon compounds, including graphene, carbon nanotubes, MOF carbon, and crystalline carbon, have been thoroughly studied. Because of its many qualities, such as its high biocompatibility, low toxicity, improved mechanical and thermal stability, exceptional water dispersion ability, and ease of functionalization, carbon quantum dots have become a viable and sustainable alternative for use in drug transport, biosensors, bioimaging, and nanomedicine [9]. A new category of carbon nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm is called carbon dots (CDs). Scientists have become interested in CDs recently due to their intriguing chemical and physical characteristics. A substitute fluorescent material for dyes is CDs. Zero-dimensional materials with comparatively significant fluorescence properties are called CDs [10]. The excitation-dependent fluorescence on CDs may be readily functionalized in a quantum yield, and they are non-toxic and biodegradable. The synthesis of CDs can be accomplished by a variety of techniques, such as electrochemical synthesis, hydrothermal, acid oxidation, laser ablation, and microwave irradiation. Hydrothermal synthesis has generally been the method of choice for many individuals. Bottom-up procedures are simple, affordable, and environmentally friendly [11].

In particular, the hydrothermal-carbonization process is simple, broadly applicable, and has a limited size distribution for the production of CDs. Green carbon has been obtained from fruit biomass, vegetables, milk, caramel, and honey, among other sources, for the synthesis of CDs [12]. Additionally, natural antecedents, including plants, roots, leaves, fruits, and waste materials that were employed as carbon sources, are used to prepare CDs [13]. Recent studies have shown that N-CDs derived from Borassus flabellifer plant extract boost catalytic activity while reducing the dye Congo-red's organic moiety [14]. The N-CDs made from plant extract Cyamopsis tetragonoloba were utilized to look into the electrocatalytic activity [15]. Quercetin was shown to include N-CDs derived from Luffa acutangula plant extract, which acted as a medication carrier and anti-cancer agent [16]. Advanced research is being done on the Pd-NCDs composite material electrocatalytic activity produced from plant extract of Morinda citrifolia. Bottom-up approach synthesis is the umbrella term for this kind of biogenic synthesis [17].

The Tylophora indica is a shrub with medicinal significance since it is known to contain a variety of chemical substances, including phenolic compounds, flavonoids, terpenoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which are spread throughout the plant's body [18]. The Tylophora indica, often known as Velvet Mesquite, is a family of plants that belongs to the Mimosoideae subfamily of these, 40 species are native to the USA, three to Asia, and one to Africa [19]. Pharmacologically potent compounds have been isolated from Tylophora indica leaves, roots, stems, branches, bark, and pollen. The Tylophora indica crude extract is reported to possess a diverse class of secondary compounds with a special combination of antifungal and therapeutic properties by using this abundant resource, there is a good chance of producing bioactive natural compounds, which might be a valuable supply of raw materials for the chemical and pharmaceutical sectors. However, metal-doped CDs have shown to be a useful tool for raising the quantum yield while producing excellent stability. The N-CDs, in particular, have increased the quantum yield, and both amine and carboxyl groups benefit from the highly stable CDs [20]. Moreover, CDs are widely utilized for their antimicrobial properties. Materials containing carbon can regulate the growth of microbes. Specifically, CDs could regulate a variety of bacterial and fungal infections. This antibacterial research can also aid in the development of antibacterial components and medications [21].

In this study, I prepared an incredibly easy and environmentally friendly technique for making NDCDs. I used Tylophora indica plant extract and a one-pot hydrothermal procedure to create high-fluorescence NDCDs, being a natural raw material, it is inexpensive and requires no processing. This has a lot of alkaloids, triterpenoids, anthraquinones, and other compounds, in addition to being ecologically friendly. The synthesized NDCDs exhibit high biocompatibility, sustained fluorescence, good water solubility, and low cost. Using sodium borohydride (NaBH4), the catalytic capacity of the prepared NDCDs were examined for reduction of Malachite Green (MG) dye. Due to the intense fluorescence of the synthesized NDCDs, printed patterns with anti-counterfeiting properties were created using fluorescent ink.

The Tylophora indica Excellent fresh leaves were gathered in India. After being trimmed, the leaves were dried in a dehumidified air dryer. To make a fine powder, the dried leaves were ground in a laboratory hammer mill. Sigma-Aldrich supplied the following chemicals: aqueous ammonia (NH₄OH), alizarin yellow (C13H9N3O5), methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), styrene (C₆H₅CH=CH₂), azobisisobutyronitrile ((CH₃)₂C]₂N₂), and toluene (C₆H₅CH₃). Without any prior preparation, only laboratory-grade (LR) chemicals and reagents were utilized. Throughout the inquiry, solutions and other experiments were made with double distilled water (DDW).

Tylophora indica was used in a one-pot hydrothermal process to create the NDCDs with slight modifications [22]. Typically, the process involved adding 5g of powdered Tylophora indica, 2.5 mL of aqueous NH3 (2 M), and 50 mL of double-distilled water to the reaction. The mixture was then moved to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was heated to 180 °C for 10 h. Dark brown solutions developed as the heating process naturally cooled to ambient temperature. The item was centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 rpm. Ultimately, the vacuum-dried dark brown solutions were obtained and kept at 4 °C for additional research.

Using a JEOL JEM 2100 (Japan) microscope, high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) pictures were captured. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum analysis was conducted using the Thermo Nicolet Corporation's (USA) NEXUS spectrometer. Using an Al Kα excitation system, the ESCALAB250Xi spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) was used to perform X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Using an Agilent Cary 100 spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), the UV–Vis absorption spectrum was measured. Using a FLS980 steady state/transient fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) and an LS-55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA), all of the fluorescence spectra and the lifespan were recorded. In the experiment, pH was measured using a PHS-2C(A) pF-meter (Shanghai Dapu Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

In order to determine the fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY) of the NDCDs, we examined the values of absorbance and integrated photoluminescence intensities of each to quinine sulfate, a reference material [23]. While the NDCDs were dispersing throughout the distilled water, quinine sulfate, which has a quantum yield (QY) of 0.54, was dissolved in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution. The following expression represents the quantum yield (Φ) calculation:
(1) Φ = ΦR × I / IR × AR / A × η2 / η2R
Here, A is the optical density (OD), η is the refractive index, I is the integrated emission intensity, and Φ is the quantum yield of the NDCDs. By comparing the NDCDs fluorescence characteristics to those of the quinine sulfate reference, we may use this equation to determine the quantum yield of the NDCDs quantitatively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the catalytic activity of synthesized NDCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The MG reduction was carried out in a quartz cuvette (3.5 mL) with a 1 cm length. Several studies were carried out in order to determine the optimal concentration of MG, NaBH4, and NDCDs (catalyst). The optimal MG and NDCD concentrations were found and fixed. 1 mL of MG (1 × 10−4 M) was combined with 0.20 mL of freshly manufactured ice-cooled NaBH4 (0.50 M) and 1.80 mL of water in the first reaction. In the second reaction, 20 mL of produced NDCDs were added to the reaction mixtures described above. Using a PerkinElmer lambda UV–vis spectrophotometer, these reactions were constantly monitored at linear time intervals.

To begin, 5 g styrene with 50 mg azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was combined in a 10 mL centrifuge tube. After that, AIBN was dissolved fully in styrene by sonicating for a length of time. The mixed solution was then maintained at 50 °C for 20 min to prepolymerize styrene for later usage. The NDCDs were then dissolved in 10 mL of toluene solution and agitated to create a homogenous white solution. To prepare fluorescent ink, 3 mL of NDCDs solution was combined with prepolymerized styrene. It is important to note that the viscosity of fluorescent ink might affect the printing effect. The viscosity of the ink can be modified to achieve the optimal fluorescent ink by varying the polymerization degree of styrene and AIBN.

The NDCDs were created utilizing a hydrothermal procedure and carefully chosen precursors and dopants. The color changed from light green to brown when it was carbonized, demonstrating the formation of carbon dots. Temperature and reaction time are important factors in the creation of fluorescent NDCDs with certain characteristics. For future use, the final purified solution encompassing the fluorescent NDCDs was kept in a freezer at less than 4 °C [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and surface state of NDCDs
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM images with the SAED pattern were used to examine the morphology and crystallinity of the prepared NDCDs. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the prepared NDCDs were homogeneously spread from one another on the carbon-coated Cu grid. The NDCDs have a mean particle size of 4.3 nm (range: 1.0–9 nm) and were spherical with a tight size distribution. Fig. 1b. The HRTEM pictures revealed lattice fringes, and the d-spacing value is 0.37 nm Fig. 1c, indicating the graphitic carbon C(002) plane. The evident circular formation in Fig. 1d indicates the amorphous nature of carbon revealed by the selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) of NDCDs [25].

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , the synthesized NDCDs Raman spectra. Both the D (defect region) and G (graphitic region) bands in the spectrum show two distinct Raman signals at around 1422 and 1631 cm−1, which are referred to as the sp3 and sp2 hybridization of carbon atoms, respectively. One could have a perfect graphite nature if the I
D and I
G ratio intensities are less than one. The I
D < I
G indicates that the intensity of I
D is less than the intensity of I
G , and the I
D and I
G ratio values for NDCDs were found to be 0.86. It also demonstrates that there are moderate surface flaws and that the synthesized NDCDs have an excellent graphite structure [7].

The optical properties of NDCDs were ascertained using the FL spectrum and UV–Vis absorption. The absorbance peak, which is located at 276 and 320 nm, is clearly visible in the UV absorption spectra. For π-π* transitions, the aromatic carbon C=C (sp2) bond produced the initial peak, which was seen at 276 nm. Fig. 3 a, the additional absorption peak at 320 nm is ascribed to the C=O/C=N bond for n-π* transitions [26]. The size, aqueous medium, physical environment, and chemical composition all have a significant impact on the absorption spectrum. It has also been shown that the absorption spectra broaden and peak intensity diminishes with decreasing particle size. The quantum size defect, electronic conjugate structures, and zig-zag sites could cause the fluorescence emission. The FL excitation spectra of the NDCDs were displayed in Fig. 3b, with 310 nm being the maximum excitation wavelength. In Fig. 3c displays the FL emission spectra of the constructed NDCDs with a range of excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. Because the excitation wavelengths were different, the FL intensity fell linearly. The FL intensity dropped as the excitation-independent wavelength was raised from 300 to 400 nm, which may have been caused by the n-π * transition brought on by the nitrogen functionalization and non-bonded electrons. The maximum emission (λmax (em)) of 393 nm was measured at the maximum excitation wavelength (λmax (ext)) of 300 nm. The constructed NDCDs excitation-dependent emission characteristics result from their various sizes. The distinctive fluorescence features of the synthesized NDCDs have led to their widespread applicability in biological and sensing applications [27]. It was discovered that the fluorescent NDCDs had a quantum yield (QY) of 16.1 %.

The produced NDCDs' structure and phase nature were ascertained using the XRD pattern. The large diffraction peak at 2θ = 22.14° is shown in Fig. 4 . The NDCDs are attributed to C(002), which corresponds to the planes of the graphitic carbon core and the amorphous nature of the carbon atoms, as well as to the nitrogen atoms' surface functionalization. For constructed NDCDs, the d-spacing value was computed using Bragg's equation, d = nλ/2sin[θ]. Where λ is the X-ray wavelength (1.54 Å), n is a positive number (1), and θ is the plane's angle. For constructed NDCDs, the interlayer distance, or d-spacing, was 0.37 nm, which is connected to the C(002) planes [28]. These findings might be linked to the interlayer distance of the lattice fringes observed in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1c).

As illustrated in Fig. 5 , the functional groups of as-prepared NDCDs were investigated using FTIR. The FTIR spectra of NDCDs showed intense peaks at 3314 cm−1 (O–H/N–H stretching), 2984 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1655 cm−1 (C=C stretching), 1381 cm−1 (O–H bending), 1084 cm−1 (C–O stretching), and 780 cm−1 (C–H bending), indicating that the functional groups of NDCDs are hydroxyl groups and sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon atoms [29]. Additionally, peaks at 1293 cm−1 (C–N stretching) and 1581 cm−1 (N–H stretching) indicate the presence of amino groups (−NH2). The C–O stretching peak at 675 cm−1 indicated that the NDCDs contained carboxylic acids as well. The FTIR spectra revealed that the duration of time employed for the synthesis procedure altered the structure of NDCDs, as extending the synthesis time can trigger the hydrolysis of precursor molecules [30]. These data indicate that boron is present on the surface of NDCDs. The FTIR spectra verified the existence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, amine, and acid groups in the produced NDCDs.

The synthesized NDCDs XPS survey scan spectrum and the high-resolution XPS spectra of each of its three elements C1s, N1s, and O1s, were displayed in Fig. 6 . Carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen peaks are located at 282.8, 400.48, and 532.27 eV, respectively (Fig. 6a). Four peaks at 285.1, 286.6, and 288.6 eV were seen in the deconvolution spectra of C1s (Fig. 6b), and they are associated with the C–H, C=C (sp2), C=O, and π-π∗ transitions [31]. Particularly, it was evident from the C1s peak at 286.6 eV that the synthesized N-CDs were primarily obtained for sp2 carbons. Two binding energy peaks at 398.39 and 400.24 eV in the high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 6c) might be attributed to the (C–N–C) and (N–H) groups on the surface of the NDCDs, respectively [32]. The O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 6d) were deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks, which corresponded to the C–O and C=O groups, respectively, at 531.54 and 532.15 eV. The XPS data amply demonstrated that the synthetic NDCDs could contain carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen; their respective atomic percentages (%) were 61.3, 14.5, and 24.2 %, confirming the doping of nitrogen and oxygen across the N-CD surface. The synthesized NDCDs were functionalized by hydroxyl, amine, and carboxyl groups, as validated by the XPS results and further supported by FTIR spectroscopic investigations.

The synthesized NDCDs have been used in an attempt to reduce the organic dye malachite green by NaBH4. Because azo groups (-N=N-) are present in Malachite Green, their unique peak at 614 nm indicates a drop in absorbance, which is how the reduction reaction was detected. Without the generated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (green catalyst), Fig. 7 depicts the reduction reaction of malachite green by reductant (NaBH4). Wherein the absorbance gradually decreased when a green catalyst was not present. The intensity of the absorption peak did not significantly change even after 90 min. This demonstrated that the thermodynamically advantageous but kinetically difficult degradation of malachite green by sodium borohydride in the absence of a green catalyst occurred at very low reaction rates. Fig. 7b, the peak intensity significantly dropped, and the reaction was finished in 18 min after the addition of 20 μL of catalyst to the vessel containing Malachite Green and sodium borohydride. This demonstrated that a decrease in activation energy may be achieved while still increasing the reaction rate with the green catalyst [33]. In the pseudo-first-order kinetics equation, the rate constant (k) for the reduction of malachite green by synthetic green catalyst was determined.
ln (At/A0) = −kt
Where the absorbance of the zero and definite times, respectively, are denoted by A0 and At. Plotting the absorbance change ln (At/A0) versus reaction time Fig. 7c revealed excellent linearity. From the slope of a linear plot, the coefficient determination (R2) and k were obtained. The K and R2 were calculated to be 0.1830 min−1 and 0.9875, respectively. With a green catalyst, the rate of reaction for the reduction of malachite green by NaBH4 was higher than previously reported. However, linearity between ln (At/A0) and time (T) was not achieved in the case of NaBH4 alone. It demonstrates that the reduction reaction of malachite green with artificial NDCDs was advantageous in terms of both kinetics and thermodynamics. It was discovered that the green catalyst lowered the activation energy and quickened the rate of reaction [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible reduction mechanism of malachite green
ce:section.ce:para: The proposed dye reduction mechanism using NDCDs and NaBH4 as a reducing agent. The well-known Langmuir-Hinshelwood (LH) model might assist explain this, as seen in Fig. 8 . The interaction between dyes and NaBH4 involved charge transfer, with the Malachite Green dye molecules functioning as electron acceptors and the NaBH4 molecules acting as electron donors [35]. However, in the absence of a catalyst (NDCDs), the reduction of Malachite Green dye in the presence of NaBH4 was kinetically regulated due to their large potential difference. When NaBH4 is decomposed, the borohydride (BH4 −) ion is liberated and bound to the surface of the NDCDs. The BH4 − ion will make a covalent link with the catalyst's surface. Similarly, the dyes could be added to the surface of NDCD catalysts. Following that, the nucleophile BH4 − ions give electrons to dye molecules via green nano-catalysts (NDCDs), which serve as an electron relay system [36]. Next, receiving electrons via catalysts and H2 from hydride (BH4 −) ions, the dye molecules were reduced and then desorbed from the catalysts, allowing the reduction reaction to continue. When Malachite Green is reduced with NaBH4, the azo group (-N=N-) is converted into an amine group. This depicts how the generated NDCDs alter NaBH4 catalytic activity during malachite green breakdown. In the presence of NDCDs, Fig. 9 shows the structural changes of the initial Malachite Green and Leuco Malachite Green after reduction [37].

These NDCDs solutions are applied as smart and security fluorescent inks for anti-counterfeiting applications as an additional proof-of-concept demonstration [38]. For this experimental design, corrosion-resistant ink cartridges and non-fluorescent paper were selected. Information encryption is achieved by printing the NDCDs solution as inks onto non-fluorescent paper using inkjet printing, as seen in Fig. 10 a. The encrypted data is not visible in normal lighting conditions [39]. Through UV radiation, the design of a blue “flower” becomes visible. In this manner, the decryption of the printed information can be managed by using the UV light as a switch. The stability of printed patterns is critical for applications involving information encryption and anti-counterfeiting [40]. The encrypted paper with a “flower” design is kept for 30 days in ambient air at 22 °C and 40 % humidity, as seen in Fig. 10b. After the 30-day storage period, the “flower” design is still discernible and decrypted when exposed to UV light. The paper with a printed “flower” pattern is heated to 60 °C and held there for 10 min to show the heat-resistance of NDCD's fluorescent inks for use in anti-counterfeiting applications Fig. 10c. The blue “flower” design was still discernible, despite a little decrease in brightness. The brilliant blue “flower” pattern returns after the paper cools naturally to room temperature, suggesting that these NDCD fluorescent inks have good heat resilience for use in upcoming information security applications [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability of the NDCDs fluorescent ink
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the photostability of the synthetic NDCDs fluorescent ink, we captured FL spectra when the ink was exposed to 365 nm illumination throughout a range of periods. Plot (a) confirms that the illumination of NDCDs ink underwent photobleaching in less than 25 min. In addition, the NDCDs' ink displays the plot values even after 2 h of photoexcitation at 365 nm (b). According to the photostability investigation, NDCDs with light exhibited longer stability than those without light, as seen in Fig. 11 . The photos that were taken with NDCDs ink exposed to UV light (b) and without (a) are shown in the inset Fig. 11. Because of NDCDs fluorescent ink's remarkable photostability, inexpensive raw material costs, and simple synthetic process, we believe it to be a promising material for fluorescent ink production [42]. This observation led to the effective use of the synthesized NDCDs as an anti-counterfeiting agent.

The present study demonstrates the effective manufacture of a green catalyst using nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Tylophora indica extract using a one-step hydrothermal carbonization process. The spherical nature of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NDCDs) and their average size of around 4.3 nm are confirmed by HR-TEM. With the use of XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy, the chemical compositions and surface functional groups were identified. The produced NDCDs were proven to be well graphitized by the I
D /I
G ratio 0.86 obtained by Raman spectroscopy tests. The produced NDCDs released excellent fluorescence at an excitation wavelength of 310 nm. The UV–visible spectroscopy, it was discovered that the produced nitrogen-doped carbon dots possessed a remarkable catalytic capacity for the reduction of malachite green by NaBH4. The calculated rate constant for the reduction of malachite green is 0.1830 min−1, indicating that the synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon dots have enhanced catalytic activity. Additionally, the NDCDs solution is used as a strong fluorescent ink for applications that prevent counterfeiting. The current work suggests a practical method for producing pure-phase NDCDs with good FLQY, which can aid in the future development of highly effective metal-free nanomaterials.

Mir Waqas Alam: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Deanship of Scientific Research, Vice Presidency for Graduate Studies and Scientific Research, King Faisal University, Saudi Arabia [Project No: GRANT5427].

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Degradation of hazardous organic dyes in water by nanomaterials
Environ. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shanker, U., Rani, M. and Jassal, V., 2017. Degradation of hazardous organic dyes in water by nanomaterials. Environmental chemistry letters, 15, pp.623-642.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of Ni-Cu-Zn based nanosized metal oxides for photocatalytic and sensor applications
Crystals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alam MW, Aamir M, Farhan M, Albuhulayqah M, Ahmad MM, Ravikumar CR, Dileep Kumar VG, Ananda Murthy HC. Green Synthesis of Ni-Cu-Zn Based Nanosized Metal Oxides for Photocatalytic and Sensor Applications. Crystals. 2021; 11(12):1467. https://doi.org/10.3390/cryst11121467
